name: Aggregate Capacity Warning
description: Detects aggregates with used capacity exceeding 85%. High aggregate utilization leads to performance degradation due to reduced free space for WAFL write optimization and may result in out-of-space conditions if growth continues unchecked.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 AS used_pct, size_bytes, used_bytes, available_bytes FROM ontap_aggregates WHERE system_id = ? AND CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 > 85 AND CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 <= 95
condition: Aggregate used capacity is between 85% and 95%
impact: Performance degradation due to reduced free space for WAFL write allocation. Risk of aggregate becoming full if growth rate is not managed, potentially causing volume write failures and application outages.
remediation_steps:
1. Review aggregate utilization: storage aggregate show -fields size,used,available,percent-used
2. Identify volumes consuming the most space: volume show -aggregate <aggr> -fields size,used,percent-used -sort-by percent-used
3. Consider enabling FabricPool tiering for cold data: storage aggregate object-store attach -aggregate <aggr> -object-store-name <store>
4. Add disks to the aggregate: storage aggregate add-disks -aggregate <aggr> -diskcount <n>
5. Move volumes to less-utilized aggregates: volume move start -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -destination-aggregate <target_aggr>
6. Delete unnecessary snapshots: volume snapshot delete -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -snapshot <snap>
support_case_note: Open a support case if aggregate capacity is growing unexpectedly fast or if adding disks does not resolve the issue due to RAID group constraints.
kb_articles: KB-1013656: Best practices for aggregate space management

name: Aggregate Capacity Critical
description: Detects aggregates with used capacity exceeding 95%. Critically full aggregates can cause write failures across all hosted volumes, leading to application outages, data corruption, and failed snapshots.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 AS used_pct, size_bytes, used_bytes, available_bytes FROM ontap_aggregates WHERE system_id = ? AND CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 > 95
condition: Aggregate used capacity exceeds 95%
impact: Imminent risk of write failures on all volumes in the aggregate. Applications will experience I/O errors. Snapshots will fail. SnapMirror transfers may abort. If the aggregate reaches 100%, the node may become unresponsive.
remediation_steps:
1. IMMEDIATE: Identify and delete unnecessary snapshots: volume snapshot delete -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -snapshot <snap>
2. IMMEDIATE: Check for orphaned snapshot copies: volume snapshot show -aggregate <aggr> -fields create-time,size
3. Move volumes to other aggregates: volume move start -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -destination-aggregate <target_aggr>
4. Add disks to the aggregate: storage aggregate add-disks -aggregate <aggr> -diskcount <n>
5. Review and reduce snapshot reserve: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -percent-snapshot-space <pct>
6. Enable volume autodelete for snapshots: volume snapshot autodelete modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -enabled true -trigger snap_reserve
support_case_note: Open a support case immediately if the aggregate is at 98%+ and no space can be freed, or if write failures are occurring.
kb_articles: KB-1013656: Best practices for aggregate space management
KB-1014136: How to recover from an out-of-space condition

name: Volume Capacity Warning
description: Detects volumes with used capacity exceeding 90%. Volumes approaching capacity limits may cause application write failures if autogrow is not enabled or the aggregate lacks space for growth.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 AS used_pct, size_bytes, used_bytes, available_bytes, state, type FROM ontap_volumes WHERE system_id = ? AND type = 'rw' AND CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 > 90 AND CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 <= 95
condition: Read-write volume used capacity is between 90% and 95%
impact: Risk of write failures if the volume reaches capacity. Applications may experience slowdowns as the volume approaches full. Snapshot creation may fail.
remediation_steps:
1. Check volume space details: volume show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -fields size,used,available,percent-used,autosize-mode
2. Enable or adjust autogrow: volume autosize -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -mode grow -maximum-size <max_size>
3. Increase volume size manually: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -size +<size>g
4. Archive or delete unused data
5. Review and clean old snapshots: volume snapshot show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -fields size,create-time
6. Consider enabling deduplication: volume efficiency on -vserver <svm> -volume <vol>
support_case_note: Open a support case if autogrow is enabled but the volume is not growing as expected.
kb_articles: KB-1015040: Volume space management best practices

name: Volume Capacity Critical
description: Detects volumes with used capacity exceeding 95%. Critically full volumes will cause application write failures, potential data corruption, and may trigger cascading failures in dependent services.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 AS used_pct, size_bytes, used_bytes, available_bytes, state, type FROM ontap_volumes WHERE system_id = ? AND type = 'rw' AND CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 > 95
condition: Read-write volume used capacity exceeds 95%
impact: Application write failures are imminent or already occurring. Database transactions may fail. Snapshot creation will fail. SnapMirror relationships will break. Risk of data corruption if applications do not handle write failures gracefully.
remediation_steps:
1. IMMEDIATE: Increase volume size: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -size +<size>g
2. IMMEDIATE: Delete unnecessary snapshots: volume snapshot delete -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -snapshot <snap>
3. Enable autogrow with adequate max size: volume autosize -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -mode grow -maximum-size <max_size>
4. Check aggregate free space to ensure growth is possible: storage aggregate show -fields available
5. Move volume to larger aggregate if needed: volume move start -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -destination-aggregate <target_aggr>
6. Enable snapshot autodelete as a safety net: volume snapshot autodelete modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -enabled true
support_case_note: Open a support case if the volume cannot be expanded and data loss is occurring.
kb_articles: KB-1015040: Volume space management best practices
KB-1014136: How to recover from an out-of-space condition

name: Snapshot Reserve Overflow
description: Detects volumes where snapshot consumption has exceeded the configured snapshot reserve, spilling into the active filesystem data space. This silently reduces the space available for user data and can lead to unexpected out-of-space conditions.
check_query: SELECT v.system_id, v.name, v.snapshot_reserve_pct, CAST(COALESCE(SUM(s.size_bytes), 0) AS REAL) / NULLIF(v.size_bytes, 0) * 100 AS snap_used_pct FROM ontap_volumes v LEFT JOIN ontap_snapshots s ON v.system_id = s.system_id AND v.id = s.volume_id WHERE v.system_id = ? AND v.type = 'rw' GROUP BY v.system_id, v.id, v.name, v.snapshot_reserve_pct, v.size_bytes HAVING snap_used_pct > v.snapshot_reserve_pct
condition: Volume snapshot usage percentage exceeds the configured snapshot reserve percentage
impact: Active filesystem space is being consumed by snapshots beyond the reserve allocation. Users will see less available space than expected. Volume may reach full capacity faster than projected.
remediation_steps:
1. Review snapshot usage: volume snapshot show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -fields size,create-time
2. Delete old or unnecessary snapshots: volume snapshot delete -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -snapshot <snap>
3. Increase snapshot reserve: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -percent-snapshot-space <new_pct>
4. Review snapshot policies for appropriate retention: volume snapshot policy show
5. Consider enabling snapshot autodelete: volume snapshot autodelete modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -enabled true -trigger snap_reserve
6. Investigate applications or backup tools creating excessive snapshots
support_case_note: Open a support case if snapshots cannot be deleted due to SnapMirror or SnapLock dependencies.
kb_articles: KB-1014224: Understanding snapshot reserve and overflow

name: Root Volume High Capacity
description: Detects root volumes with used capacity exceeding 80%. The root volume contains critical node configuration data including core files, boot data, and audit logs. A full root volume can prevent node boot and cause cluster instability.
check_query: SELECT v.system_id, v.name, CAST(v.used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(v.size_bytes, 0) * 100 AS used_pct, v.size_bytes, v.used_bytes FROM ontap_volumes v INNER JOIN ontap_aggregates a ON v.aggregate_id = a.id WHERE v.system_id = ? AND a.is_root = 1 AND v.type = 'rw' AND CAST(v.used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(v.size_bytes, 0) * 100 > 80
condition: Root volume used capacity exceeds 80%
impact: Node may fail to boot if the root volume becomes full. Core dumps cannot be saved. Audit logs may stop recording. Cluster configuration changes may fail.
remediation_steps:
1. Check root volume usage: volume show -volume vol0 -fields size,used,available,percent-used
2. Clean up core dump files: system node coredump delete-all -node <node>
3. Remove old audit log files if stored on root volume
4. Increase root volume size: volume modify -vserver <node_svm> -volume vol0 -size +<size>g
5. Ensure only essential data resides on the root aggregate: volume show -aggregate <root_aggr>
6. Move non-essential volumes off the root aggregate
support_case_note: Open a support case if the root volume is above 95% and cannot be cleaned up, or if the node is experiencing boot issues.
kb_articles: KB-1013496: Root volume best practices and management

name: Metadata Volume Low Space
description: Detects MDV (metadata) volumes with used capacity exceeding 85%. Metadata volumes store SVM root directory data, audit logs, and other critical SVM-level metadata. Low space on MDV volumes can prevent SVM operations.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 AS used_pct, size_bytes, used_bytes FROM ontap_volumes WHERE system_id = ? AND (name LIKE 'MDV_%' OR name LIKE 'mdv_%') AND CAST(used_bytes AS REAL) / NULLIF(size_bytes, 0) * 100 > 85
condition: Metadata volume (MDV_*) used capacity exceeds 85%
impact: SVM operations may fail. New volume creation may be blocked. Audit logging may stop. CIFS/NFS junction path resolution may fail.
remediation_steps:
1. Identify metadata volumes: volume show -volume MDV_* -fields size,used,available,percent-used
2. Check what is consuming space within the metadata volume
3. Increase metadata volume size: volume modify -vserver <admin_svm> -volume <mdv_vol> -size +<size>g
4. Ensure the hosting aggregate has sufficient free space
5. Contact NetApp Support if metadata volume contents are unclear
support_case_note: Open a support case if MDV volumes are consistently filling up or if SVM operations are failing due to MDV space issues. NetApp Support can help identify the root cause.

name: Inode Utilization High
description: Detects volumes where inode utilization exceeds 90% by comparing the file count metrics. When a volume runs out of inodes, no new files can be created even if free space exists. This is particularly impactful for volumes hosting many small files.
check_query: SELECT m.system_id, m.resource_id AS name, m.avg_value AS inode_used_pct FROM metrics_hourly m WHERE m.system_id = ? AND m.resource_type = 'volume' AND m.metric_name = 'inode_utilization' AND m.avg_value > 90 AND m.hour_timestamp >= datetime('now', '-4 hours') GROUP BY m.system_id, m.resource_id HAVING MAX(m.hour_timestamp)
condition: Volume inode utilization exceeds 90% based on hourly metrics
impact: File creation will fail when inodes are exhausted, even if disk space is available. Applications will receive 'No space left on device' errors. This is especially critical for email, log storage, and small-file workloads.
remediation_steps:
1. Check inode count: volume show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -fields files,files-used
2. Increase maximum file (inode) count: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -files <new_max>
3. Note: Increasing inode count requires available aggregate space
4. Identify directories with excessive file counts: volume file show-inode -vserver <svm> -volume <vol>
5. Archive or remove unneeded small files
6. Consider splitting workloads across multiple volumes
support_case_note: Open a support case if inode limits cannot be increased or if the volume is exhibiting unexpected inode consumption patterns.
kb_articles: KB-1010376: How to increase the inode count on a FlexVol volume

name: Node Down or Degraded
description: Detects ONTAP nodes reporting an unhealthy status. An unhealthy node indicates hardware failures, subsystem errors, or critical service disruptions that affect data availability and cluster stability.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, model, serial_number, uptime FROM ontap_nodes WHERE system_id = ? AND is_healthy = 0
condition: Node is_healthy flag is false (0)
impact: Data served by the node may be unavailable if HA takeover has not occurred. If HA partner is also unhealthy, data loss is possible. Cluster quorum may be at risk in smaller clusters. All volumes and LIFs hosted on this node are affected.
remediation_steps:
1. Check node health status: system health status show
2. Check HA status: storage failover show
3. Check subsystem health: system health subsystem show
4. Review EMS logs for root cause: event log show -severity EMERGENCY,ALERT,ERROR -node <node>
5. If HA partner is available and healthy, consider planned takeover: storage failover takeover -ofnode <node>
6. Check hardware health: system node environment sensors show -node <node>
7. If node is completely down, check physical connectivity and power
support_case_note: Open a P1 support case immediately if a node is down and HA takeover has not occurred. Provide EMS logs, AutoSupport data, and system health output.

name: HA Partner Not Available
description: Detects HA pair configurations where the partner node is unhealthy or in takeover state. When HA is compromised, the cluster loses its ability to provide transparent failover, creating a single point of failure.
check_query: SELECT n1.system_id, n1.name AS node_name, n1.is_healthy AS node_healthy FROM ontap_nodes n1 WHERE n1.system_id = ? AND n1.is_healthy = 0 AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ontap_nodes n2 WHERE n2.system_id = n1.system_id AND n2.cluster_id = n1.cluster_id AND n2.id != n1.id)
condition: A node in an HA pair reports unhealthy status while a partner node exists
impact: Loss of HA redundancy means any further failure on the remaining node will result in a complete outage. No transparent failover is available. Data protection is significantly degraded.
remediation_steps:
1. Check HA pair status: storage failover show
2. Check interconnect status: storage failover show -fields partner-name,partner-status,state
3. Verify HA mailbox disks: storage failover mailbox-disk show
4. Check for takeover state: storage failover show -instance
5. If partner is in takeover state and issue is resolved, perform giveback: storage failover giveback -ofnode <node>
6. Check EMS for HA-related events: event log show -message-name *ha* -severity EMERGENCY,ALERT,ERROR
support_case_note: Open a P1 support case if HA partner cannot be recovered. Include AutoSupport output and HA status details.

name: HA Interconnect Down
description: Detects HA interconnect failures by monitoring for relevant EMS events. The HA interconnect carries NVRAM mirror traffic between HA partners. A failed interconnect prevents HA takeover and puts data integrity at risk.
check_query: SELECT system_id, node, severity, message_name, message_text, time FROM ontap_ems_events WHERE system_id = ? AND (message_name LIKE '%ha.ic%' OR message_name LIKE '%ic.offline%' OR message_name LIKE '%interconnect%down%') AND severity IN ('emergency', 'alert', 'error') AND time >= datetime('now', '-24 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate HA interconnect failure or offline status within the last 24 hours
impact: NVRAM mirroring between HA partners is disrupted. In the event of a node failure, recent writes may be lost. HA takeover may be disabled or restricted. Data integrity is compromised.
remediation_steps:
1. Check HA interconnect status: storage failover show -fields interconnect-status
2. Verify interconnect hardware: system ha interconnect show
3. Check for cable issues on HA interconnect ports
4. Review EMS logs: event log show -message-name *ic* -severity EMERGENCY,ALERT,ERROR
5. Reseat HA interconnect cables if physical access is available
6. If using external HA interconnect switch, check switch status
7. If internal interconnect (newer platforms), contact NetApp Support
support_case_note: Open a P1 support case immediately. HA interconnect failures typically indicate hardware issues requiring field service. Provide platform model, serial number, and EMS logs.

name: Aggregate Offline or Degraded
description: Detects aggregates that are not in an online state. Offline or degraded aggregates make all contained volumes inaccessible, causing widespread application outages.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, state, raid_type, disk_count FROM ontap_aggregates WHERE system_id = ? AND state != 'online'
condition: Aggregate state is not 'online'
impact: All volumes in the aggregate are inaccessible. Applications using those volumes will experience complete outages. SnapMirror relationships sourcing from these volumes will fail. LUN access will be lost for SAN workloads.
remediation_steps:
1. Check aggregate state: storage aggregate show -aggregate <aggr> -instance
2. Check RAID group status: storage aggregate show-status -aggregate <aggr>
3. Check for failed disks: storage disk show -broken
4. If aggregate is degraded (RAID reconstructing), monitor progress: storage aggregate show-status -aggregate <aggr>
5. Attempt to bring aggregate online: storage aggregate online -aggregate <aggr>
6. If disks are failed, verify spares are available: storage disk show -container-type spare
7. Check EMS for RAID events: event log show -message-name *raid* -severity EMERGENCY,ALERT,ERROR
support_case_note: Open a P1 support case if the aggregate is offline and cannot be brought online, or if multiple disks have failed simultaneously (double disk failure).

name: Volume Offline
description: Detects ONTAP volumes that are in an offline state. Offline volumes are inaccessible to clients and applications, causing service disruptions for all workloads depending on the volume.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, state, type, style FROM ontap_volumes WHERE system_id = ? AND state = 'offline'
condition: Volume state is 'offline'
impact: All data on the volume is inaccessible. NFS/CIFS shares are unavailable. LUNs become inaccessible to hosts. SnapMirror relationships will report errors. Dependent applications will experience outages.
remediation_steps:
1. Check volume state: volume show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -instance
2. Check if the hosting aggregate is online: storage aggregate show -aggregate <aggr>
3. Bring volume online: volume online -vserver <svm> -volume <vol>
4. Verify junction path is mounted: volume show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -fields junction-path
5. Re-mount if needed: volume mount -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -junction-path <path>
6. Check EMS for volume-related errors: event log show -message-name *vol* -severity EMERGENCY,ALERT,ERROR
7. Verify client access after bringing online
support_case_note: Open a support case if the volume cannot be brought online or if it repeatedly goes offline without administrative action.

name: LIF Not on Home Port
description: Detects logical interfaces (LIFs) that have migrated away from their home node or port. LIFs not on their home port cause suboptimal data paths, increased latency, and potential performance degradation as traffic traverses the cluster interconnect.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, ip_address, home_node, home_port, current_node, current_port, role FROM ontap_lifs WHERE system_id = ? AND (home_node != current_node OR home_port != current_port)
condition: LIF current_node differs from home_node or current_port differs from home_port
impact: Data access follows a suboptimal path through the cluster interconnect, increasing latency. Network bandwidth on the home port is underutilized. In a multi-path failure scenario, fewer failover targets are available.
remediation_steps:
1. Review LIF placement: network interface show -fields home-node,home-port,curr-node,curr-port,status-oper,is-home
2. Revert a specific LIF: network interface revert -vserver <svm> -lif <lif_name>
3. Revert all non-home LIFs: network interface revert -vserver * -lif *
4. Investigate why the LIF failed over: Check port status on home port
5. Verify home port is operational: network port show -node <home_node> -port <home_port>
6. Set auto-revert for future protection: network interface modify -vserver <svm> -lif <lif_name> -auto-revert true
support_case_note: Open a support case if LIFs repeatedly migrate away from home and the underlying port/node issue cannot be identified.
kb_articles: KB-1013637: LIF management and auto-revert configuration

name: LIF Operationally Down
description: Detects logical interfaces that are operationally down. A down LIF means clients cannot access data through that network path, potentially causing application outages if no alternative paths exist.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, ip_address, home_node, home_port, current_node, current_port, role, operational_status FROM ontap_lifs WHERE system_id = ? AND operational_status = 'down'
condition: LIF operational_status is 'down'
impact: Clients connected through this LIF lose access to data. NFS mounts may hang. CIFS sessions will be disrupted. iSCSI paths will fail. If this is the only LIF for a SVM, complete data access loss occurs.
remediation_steps:
1. Check LIF status: network interface show -lif <lif_name> -instance
2. Check underlying port status: network port show -node <home_node> -port <home_port>
3. Check VLAN status if applicable: network port vlan show
4. Bring LIF up: network interface modify -vserver <svm> -lif <lif_name> -status-admin up
5. If port is down, troubleshoot physical connectivity: network port show -node <node> -fields link,health
6. Check failover configuration: network interface show -failover -lif <lif_name>
7. Verify DNS resolution for the LIF IP address
support_case_note: Open a support case if LIFs are down due to suspected hardware failure of the network adapter or port.

name: Network Port Down
description: Detects physical or VLAN network ports that are in a down state. Down ports reduce network redundancy and may cause LIF failovers, leading to suboptimal data paths.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, type, speed, state, broadcast_domain FROM ontap_ports WHERE system_id = ? AND state = 'down'
condition: Network port state is 'down'
impact: Reduced network redundancy. LIFs hosted on this port will fail over to alternate ports. If no failover target is available, data access is lost. LACP/ifgrp integrity may be compromised.
remediation_steps:
1. Check port status details: network port show -node <node> -port <port> -instance
2. Verify cable connectivity and switch port status
3. Check ifgrp membership if applicable: network port ifgrp show
4. Check for CRC errors or link flaps: network port show -node <node> -port <port> -fields health-status
5. Bring port up administratively: network port modify -node <node> -port <port> -up-admin true
6. If a cable issue, replace cable and verify link comes up
7. Check switch-side port configuration (speed, duplex, VLAN)
support_case_note: Open a support case if the port shows down despite good cabling, suggesting a possible NIC hardware failure.

name: FC Port Offline
description: Detects Fibre Channel ports that are in an offline or down state. FC port failures disrupt SAN connectivity and may cause LUN access failures for connected hosts.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, type, state, broadcast_domain FROM ontap_ports WHERE system_id = ? AND type = 'fibre-channel' AND state != 'up'
condition: Fibre Channel port state is not 'up'
impact: SAN hosts connected through this FC port lose access to mapped LUNs. MPIO path count is reduced. If all paths to a LUN fail, the host experiences I/O errors and potential data corruption. ESXi hosts may show PDL (Permanent Device Loss) conditions.
remediation_steps:
1. Check FC port status: fcp adapter show -node <node> -adapter <adapter> -instance
2. Check FC port statistics: fcp adapter show -node <node> -adapter <adapter> -fields operational-status,physical-status
3. Verify FC cable and SFP: system health alert show
4. Bring FC adapter online: fcp adapter modify -node <node> -adapter <adapter> -status-admin up
5. Check zoning on FC switch to ensure proper zone configuration
6. Verify host-side MPIO paths: Run host multipath tools
7. Check for link flaps in EMS: event log show -message-name *fcp* -severity EMERGENCY,ALERT,ERROR
support_case_note: Open a support case if the FC port will not come online after cable/SFP replacement, indicating possible HBA failure.

name: Management LIF Unreachable
description: Detects cluster or node management LIFs that are operationally down. Without management LIF connectivity, the cluster cannot be managed remotely, AutoSupport cannot transmit, and monitoring tools lose visibility.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, ip_address, home_node, home_port, operational_status, role FROM ontap_lifs WHERE system_id = ? AND role IN ('cluster-mgmt', 'node-mgmt', 'management') AND operational_status = 'down'
condition: Management or cluster-mgmt LIF operational_status is 'down'
impact: Remote management access is lost. ONTAP System Manager, CLI via SSH, and REST API are unavailable. AutoSupport transmissions will fail. Monitoring and alerting systems lose connectivity. SNMP traps cannot be sent.
remediation_steps:
1. If console access is available, check management LIF: network interface show -role cluster-mgmt,node-mgmt
2. Check underlying port: network port show -node <node> -port <mgmt_port>
3. Verify management network switch connectivity
4. Bring management LIF up: network interface modify -vserver <admin_svm> -lif <mgmt_lif> -status-admin up
5. If LIF has failed over, revert to home: network interface revert -vserver <admin_svm> -lif <mgmt_lif>
6. Verify routing: network route show -vserver <admin_svm>
7. Test connectivity: network ping -node <node> -destination <gateway_ip>
support_case_note: Open a support case if management access cannot be restored and console access is the only option.

name: SnapMirror Relationship Unhealthy
description: Detects SnapMirror relationships that are not in a healthy state. Unhealthy SnapMirror relationships mean replication is not functioning and disaster recovery protection is compromised.
check_query: SELECT system_id, source_path, destination_path, state, relationship_type, policy FROM ontap_snapmirror WHERE system_id = ? AND healthy = 0
condition: SnapMirror relationship healthy flag is false (0)
impact: Data replication to the disaster recovery site has stopped. RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is not being met. In a disaster scenario, the DR copy may be significantly behind or inconsistent. Compliance requirements for data protection may be violated.
remediation_steps:
1. Check SnapMirror status: snapmirror show -destination-path <dest_path> -instance
2. Check relationship health details: snapmirror show -destination-path <dest_path> -fields healthy,unhealthy-reason
3. Resynchronize the relationship: snapmirror resync -destination-path <dest_path>
4. If resync fails, check network connectivity between clusters: cluster peer show
5. Verify intercluster LIFs are operational: network interface show -role intercluster
6. Check for snapshot conflicts: snapmirror show -destination-path <dest_path> -fields newest-snapshot
7. If relationship is broken, re-establish: snapmirror initialize -destination-path <dest_path>
support_case_note: Open a support case if SnapMirror resync repeatedly fails or if the relationship shows unknown errors.

name: SnapMirror Lag Exceeds 24 Hours
description: Detects SnapMirror relationships with lag time exceeding 24 hours (86400 seconds). Excessive lag means the disaster recovery copy is significantly behind the production data, increasing the potential data loss window.
check_query: SELECT system_id, source_path, destination_path, lag_time, state, policy FROM ontap_snapmirror WHERE system_id = ? AND CAST(lag_time AS INTEGER) > 86400 AND CAST(lag_time AS INTEGER) <= 259200 AND healthy = 1
condition: SnapMirror lag time exceeds 86400 seconds (24 hours) but is within 259200 seconds (72 hours)
impact: RPO exceeds 24 hours. In a disaster, up to 24+ hours of data changes could be lost. Compliance requirements for maximum RPO may be violated. The longer the lag, the longer resync will take.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current lag and transfer status: snapmirror show -destination-path <dest_path> -fields lag-time,transfer-schedule,state
2. Check for failed transfers: snapmirror show -destination-path <dest_path> -fields last-transfer-error
3. Trigger manual update: snapmirror update -destination-path <dest_path>
4. Verify intercluster bandwidth: network interface show -role intercluster -fields throughput-policy
5. Review transfer schedule frequency: snapmirror policy show -policy <policy>
6. Check for network throttling: snapmirror show -destination-path <dest_path> -fields throttle
7. Monitor transfer progress: snapmirror show -destination-path <dest_path> -fields transfer-bytes,transfer-duration
support_case_note: Open a support case if transfers are consistently failing or if intercluster network issues cannot be resolved.

name: SnapMirror Lag Exceeds 72 Hours
description: Detects SnapMirror relationships with lag time exceeding 72 hours (259200 seconds). This represents a severe data protection gap indicating that replication has likely been broken for an extended period.
check_query: SELECT system_id, source_path, destination_path, lag_time, state, policy FROM ontap_snapmirror WHERE system_id = ? AND CAST(lag_time AS INTEGER) > 259200
condition: SnapMirror lag time exceeds 259200 seconds (72 hours)
impact: Severe data protection gap. In a disaster, 72+ hours of data changes would be lost. The DR copy may require a full re-baseline if the common snapshot has been deleted. Regulatory compliance is likely violated.
remediation_steps:
1. IMMEDIATE: Assess the relationship state: snapmirror show -destination-path <dest_path> -instance
2. Check if common snapshot still exists: snapmirror show -destination-path <dest_path> -fields newest-snapshot
3. If common snapshot exists, resync: snapmirror resync -destination-path <dest_path>
4. If common snapshot is lost, re-baseline: snapmirror delete -destination-path <dest_path> && snapmirror release -destination-path <dest_path> && snapmirror create/initialize
5. Investigate root cause: network issues, schedule misconfiguration, or source volume changes
6. Verify intercluster LIFs and peering: cluster peer show && network interface show -role intercluster
7. Consider increasing intercluster bandwidth or enabling network compression: snapmirror modify -destination-path <dest_path> -is-network-compression-enabled true
support_case_note: Open a P2 support case for extended SnapMirror lag. If re-baseline is required, NetApp Support can assist with optimizing the initial transfer.

name: No Snapshots on Volume
description: Detects read-write data volumes that have no snapshot copies. Volumes without snapshots have no point-in-time recovery capability, meaning accidental deletions or corruptions cannot be easily recovered.
check_query: SELECT v.system_id, v.name, v.type, v.state FROM ontap_volumes v WHERE v.system_id = ? AND v.type = 'rw' AND v.state = 'online' AND v.name NOT LIKE 'vol0%' AND v.name NOT LIKE 'MDV_%' AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ontap_snapshots s WHERE s.system_id = v.system_id AND s.volume_id = v.id)
condition: Read-write online volume has zero snapshot copies (excluding root and metadata volumes)
impact: No point-in-time recovery available. Accidental file deletions cannot be recovered from .snapshot directory. SnapMirror cannot function without a base snapshot. Ransomware recovery options are limited.
remediation_steps:
1. Check snapshot policy assignment: volume show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -fields snapshot-policy
2. Assign a snapshot policy: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -snapshot-policy default
3. Create an immediate snapshot: volume snapshot create -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -snapshot <snap_name>
4. Review available snapshot policies: volume snapshot policy show
5. Ensure snapshot reserve is configured: volume show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -fields percent-snapshot-space
6. Verify .snapshot directory is accessible to clients: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -snap-dir-access true
support_case_note: No support case required unless snapshot creation fails with errors.

name: Snapshot Policy Not Applied
description: Detects read-write data volumes that do not have a snapshot policy assigned or have the 'none' policy. Without a snapshot policy, automatic snapshot creation does not occur, leaving the volume without scheduled local protection.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, type, state FROM ontap_volumes WHERE system_id = ? AND type = 'rw' AND state = 'online' AND name NOT LIKE 'vol0%' AND name NOT LIKE 'MDV_%' AND (snapshot_reserve_pct = 0 OR snapshot_reserve_pct IS NULL)
condition: Read-write online volume has snapshot reserve set to 0% or NULL (indicating no snapshot policy configured)
impact: No automatic snapshots are being created. The volume lacks scheduled local data protection. Recovery from user errors or application issues requires external backup solutions only.
remediation_steps:
1. Review current snapshot policy: volume show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -fields snapshot-policy
2. List available policies: volume snapshot policy show
3. Assign an appropriate policy: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -snapshot-policy default
4. Set snapshot reserve: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -percent-snapshot-space 5
5. Create a custom policy if needed: volume snapshot policy create -vserver <svm> -policy <policy_name> -schedule1 hourly -count1 6 -schedule2 daily -count2 2 -schedule3 weekly -count3 2
support_case_note: No support case required.

name: MetroCluster Health Check Failure
description: Detects MetroCluster health issues by monitoring EMS events related to MetroCluster (MCC) failures. MetroCluster provides synchronous replication across sites; any health issue can compromise zero-RPO disaster recovery capability.
check_query: SELECT system_id, node, severity, message_name, message_text, time FROM ontap_ems_events WHERE system_id = ? AND (message_name LIKE '%mcc%' OR message_name LIKE '%metrocluster%') AND severity IN ('emergency', 'alert', 'error') AND time >= datetime('now', '-24 hours')
condition: EMS events with MetroCluster-related messages of emergency, alert, or error severity within the last 24 hours
impact: MetroCluster synchronous replication may be compromised. Zero-RPO guarantee is at risk. Site failover (switchover) capability may be impaired. In a site disaster, data loss or extended recovery time may occur.
remediation_steps:
1. Check MetroCluster status: metrocluster show
2. Run MetroCluster check: metrocluster check run
3. Review check results: metrocluster check show
4. Check node reachability: metrocluster check node show
5. Verify aggregate mirroring: metrocluster check aggregate show
6. Check ISL (Inter-Switch Link) status: metrocluster interconnect adapter show
7. Review EMS for MCC events: event log show -message-name *mcc*,*metrocluster* -severity EMERGENCY,ALERT,ERROR
support_case_note: Open a P1 support case immediately for MetroCluster health failures. MetroCluster issues require specialized NetApp expertise. Provide metrocluster check output and AutoSupport data.

name: Volume Latency Warning
description: Detects volumes with sustained average latency exceeding 20ms over recent measurement periods. Elevated latency indicates potential performance bottlenecks that may affect application response times.
check_query: SELECT m.system_id, m.resource_id AS name, ROUND(AVG(m.avg_value), 2) AS avg_latency_ms FROM metrics_hourly m WHERE m.system_id = ? AND m.resource_type = 'volume' AND m.metric_name = 'latency' AND m.avg_value > 20 AND m.hour_timestamp >= datetime('now', '-4 hours') GROUP BY m.system_id, m.resource_id HAVING AVG(m.avg_value) > 20
condition: Volume average latency exceeds 20ms sustained over the last 4 hours
impact: Application response times are degraded. Users may experience slowness. Database query times increase. SLA targets may not be met.
remediation_steps:
1. Identify the latency source: statistics show -object volume -counter avg_latency,total_ops -instance <vol>
2. Check aggregate disk busy: statistics show -object aggregate -counter disk_busy_percent -instance <aggr>
3. Check if the volume has a QoS policy limiting throughput: volume show -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -fields qos-policy-group
4. Review workload characteristics: qos statistics workload performance show
5. Consider moving the volume to a less busy aggregate: volume move start -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -destination-aggregate <target_aggr>
6. Evaluate FabricPool tiering for cold data offload
7. Check if deduplication/compression background jobs are causing contention: volume efficiency show
support_case_note: Open a support case if latency persists despite optimization efforts, as it may indicate an underlying hardware issue.

name: Volume Latency Critical
description: Detects volumes with average latency exceeding 50ms. Critical latency levels indicate severe performance problems that are likely causing application timeouts, user-facing errors, and SLA breaches.
check_query: SELECT m.system_id, m.resource_id AS name, ROUND(AVG(m.avg_value), 2) AS avg_latency_ms FROM metrics_hourly m WHERE m.system_id = ? AND m.resource_type = 'volume' AND m.metric_name = 'latency' AND m.avg_value > 50 AND m.hour_timestamp >= datetime('now', '-2 hours') GROUP BY m.system_id, m.resource_id HAVING AVG(m.avg_value) > 50
condition: Volume average latency exceeds 50ms sustained over the last 2 hours
impact: Applications are experiencing severe performance degradation. Database timeouts are likely occurring. User-facing services are degraded or failing. SLA breaches are occurring or imminent. Business operations are impacted.
remediation_steps:
1. IMMEDIATE: Identify hot volumes and workloads: qos statistics workload performance show -sort-by latency
2. Check for runaway workloads: qos statistics workload characteristics show
3. Apply QoS ceiling to noisy workloads: qos policy-group create -policy-group <pg> -max-throughput <iops>IOPS && volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -qos-policy-group <pg>
4. Check aggregate disk busy: statistics show -object aggregate -counter disk_busy_percent
5. Check node CPU: system node show -fields cpu-busy-percent
6. Move hot volumes to less busy aggregates: volume move start ...
7. Check for background tasks (vol move, snapmirror) consuming resources
8. Review WAFL cache effectiveness: statistics show -object wafl -counter cache_hit_percent
support_case_note: Open a P2 support case if critical latency persists. Provide perfstat output and workload characterization data.

name: Aggregate Disk Busy High
description: Detects aggregates with disk busy percentage exceeding 80%. High disk busy indicates the physical disks are saturated, which is the primary cause of latency for HDD-based aggregates.
check_query: SELECT m.system_id, m.resource_id AS name, ROUND(AVG(m.avg_value), 2) AS avg_disk_busy_pct FROM metrics_hourly m WHERE m.system_id = ? AND m.resource_type = 'aggregate' AND m.metric_name = 'disk_busy' AND m.avg_value > 80 AND m.hour_timestamp >= datetime('now', '-4 hours') GROUP BY m.system_id, m.resource_id HAVING AVG(m.avg_value) > 80
condition: Aggregate average disk busy exceeds 80% sustained over the last 4 hours
impact: All volumes on this aggregate experience increased latency. Write performance is particularly affected as WAFL CP processing is delayed. RAID reconstruction time increases if a disk fails during high busy periods.
remediation_steps:
1. Identify the aggregate: storage aggregate show -aggregate <aggr> -fields disk-count,raid-type
2. Check which volumes are driving I/O: qos statistics workload performance show -aggregate <aggr> -sort-by ops
3. Add disks to spread the I/O load: storage aggregate add-disks -aggregate <aggr> -diskcount <n>
4. Move hot volumes to less busy aggregates: volume move start ...
5. Enable Flash Pool (SSD caching) if available: storage aggregate add-disks -aggregate <aggr> -diskcount <n> -disk-type SSD
6. Consider FabricPool tiering to reduce read I/O to HDDs
7. Apply QoS policies to limit workloads causing excessive I/O
support_case_note: Open a support case if disk busy remains high despite optimization, as it may indicate a workload placement issue requiring advanced analysis.

name: Node CPU Utilization High
description: Detects nodes with sustained CPU utilization exceeding 80%. High CPU utilization on an ONTAP node affects all workloads served by that node and may indicate the node is approaching its performance limits.
check_query: SELECT m.system_id, m.resource_id AS name, ROUND(AVG(m.avg_value), 2) AS avg_cpu_pct FROM metrics_hourly m WHERE m.system_id = ? AND m.resource_type = 'node' AND m.metric_name = 'cpu_utilization' AND m.avg_value > 80 AND m.hour_timestamp >= datetime('now', '-4 hours') GROUP BY m.system_id, m.resource_id HAVING AVG(m.avg_value) > 80
condition: Node average CPU utilization exceeds 80% sustained over the last 4 hours
impact: All workloads on the node experience increased latency. Protocol processing is delayed. SnapMirror and background tasks are throttled. The node may stop accepting new connections under extreme CPU pressure.
remediation_steps:
1. Check CPU utilization breakdown: system node show -fields cpu-busy-percent,cpu-elapsed-time
2. Identify top workloads: qos statistics workload performance show -node <node> -sort-by ops
3. Review background job scheduling: job show -node <node>
4. Check for excessive volume move or SnapMirror activity
5. Apply QoS ceilings to limit runaway workloads
6. Rebalance workloads by moving volumes to the HA partner or other nodes
7. Evaluate whether the platform is appropriately sized for the workload
8. Consider a technology refresh if the node is consistently over-utilized
support_case_note: Open a support case if CPU remains consistently above 90%, providing perfstat data for capacity planning analysis.
kb_articles: KB-1014050: Node CPU utilization best practices

name: WAFL Cache Hit Ratio Low
description: Detects nodes where the WAFL (Write Anywhere File Layout) cache hit ratio drops below 80%. A low cache hit ratio means more read operations must go to disk, significantly increasing latency especially on HDD-based systems.
check_query: SELECT m.system_id, m.resource_id AS name, ROUND(AVG(m.avg_value), 2) AS avg_cache_hit_pct FROM metrics_hourly m WHERE m.system_id = ? AND m.resource_type = 'node' AND m.metric_name = 'wafl_cache_hit_ratio' AND m.avg_value < 80 AND m.hour_timestamp >= datetime('now', '-4 hours') GROUP BY m.system_id, m.resource_id HAVING AVG(m.avg_value) < 80
condition: Node WAFL cache hit ratio drops below 80% sustained over the last 4 hours
impact: Increased disk I/O as more reads go to physical disks. Higher latency for read-intensive workloads. Overall system performance degradation. Flash Cache and Flash Pool become more critical for maintaining acceptable performance.
remediation_steps:
1. Check cache statistics: statistics show -object wafl -counter cache_hit_percent,cache_alloc_percent -node <node>
2. Review working set size vs. available cache: system node show -fields memory-size
3. Check if Flash Cache is installed and active: system node flash-cache show
4. Consider adding Flash Cache (PAM II/Flash Cache modules): Requires hardware installation
5. Enable Flash Pool on hot aggregates: storage aggregate add-disks -aggregate <aggr> -diskcount <n> -disk-type SSD
6. Review volume access patterns for optimization opportunities
7. Consider FabricPool tiering to keep hot data in the performance tier
support_case_note: Open a support case for sizing guidance if cache hit ratio is consistently low across all nodes.
kb_articles: KB-1013911: Understanding WAFL cache and Flash Cache

name: QoS Not Configured on Production Volumes
description: Detects systems with read-write volumes that have no QoS policies configured. Without QoS, a single workload can monopolize cluster resources, causing the 'noisy neighbor' problem and impacting other workloads.
check_query: SELECT v.system_id, v.name, v.type FROM ontap_volumes v WHERE v.system_id = ? AND v.type = 'rw' AND v.state = 'online' AND v.name NOT LIKE 'vol0%' AND v.name NOT LIKE 'MDV_%' AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ontap_qos_policies q WHERE q.system_id = v.system_id)
condition: System has online read-write volumes but no QoS policies are defined
impact: Uncontrolled workloads can consume all available resources. 'Noisy neighbor' effect can degrade performance for all volumes. No ability to guarantee minimum performance for critical workloads. Performance SLAs cannot be enforced.
remediation_steps:
1. Review current workload performance: qos statistics workload performance show
2. Create adaptive QoS policy groups for different service tiers:
   - Premium: qos adaptive-policy-group create -policy-group premium -expected-iops 6144IOPS/TB -peak-iops 12288IOPS/TB -peak-iops-allocation used-space
   - Standard: qos adaptive-policy-group create -policy-group standard -expected-iops 2048IOPS/TB -peak-iops 6144IOPS/TB -peak-iops-allocation used-space
   - Bulk: qos adaptive-policy-group create -policy-group bulk -expected-iops 128IOPS/TB -peak-iops 512IOPS/TB -peak-iops-allocation used-space
3. Assign policies to volumes: volume modify -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -qos-adaptive-policy-group <policy>
4. Monitor effectiveness: qos statistics workload performance show
support_case_note: No support case required. Contact NetApp Professional Services for QoS design assistance on complex environments.
kb_articles: KB-1015060: QoS best practices for ONTAP

name: ONTAP Version Not Current
description: Detects systems running an ONTAP version that is not the current recommended release. Running older versions means missing out on security fixes, performance improvements, and new features.
check_query: SELECT s.system_id, s.version AS current_version, k.version AS recommended_version FROM ontap_software s INNER JOIN kb_software_versions k ON k.platform = 'ontap' AND k.is_recommended = 1 WHERE s.system_id = ? AND s.version != k.version
condition: Running ONTAP version does not match the recommended version in the software knowledge base
impact: Missing critical security patches and bug fixes. Known issues in older versions may cause unexpected outages. New features and performance improvements are unavailable. NetApp Support prioritizes current versions for defect resolution.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current version: system node image show
2. Check the recommended version: Review NetApp Support site for latest recommended release
3. Review release notes for upgrade path: Visit https://mysupport.netapp.com
4. Run ANDU (Automated Non-Disruptive Upgrade) pre-checks: system node image update -node * -package <url> -validate-only true
5. Schedule maintenance window for upgrade
6. Perform ANDU: system node image update -node * -package <url> -background true
7. Monitor upgrade progress: system node image show-update-progress
8. Verify post-upgrade: system node image show
support_case_note: Open a support case if the upgrade pre-checks fail or if a custom upgrade path is needed for multi-hop upgrades.

name: ONTAP Version End of Life
description: Detects systems running an ONTAP version that has reached or is approaching End of Support (EOS). EOS versions no longer receive security patches, bug fixes, or technical support, creating significant operational risk.
check_query: SELECT s.system_id, s.version AS current_version, k.end_of_support FROM ontap_software s INNER JOIN kb_software_versions k ON k.platform = 'ontap' AND k.version = s.version WHERE s.system_id = ? AND k.end_of_support IS NOT NULL AND k.end_of_support != '' AND k.end_of_support <= date('now', '+180 days')
condition: Running ONTAP version has end_of_support date within the next 180 days or already past
impact: No security patches available for newly discovered vulnerabilities. No bug fixes for known issues. NetApp Support may have limited ability to assist. Compliance audits may flag unsupported software. Insurance and regulatory requirements may not be met.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current version EOS date: Visit https://mysupport.netapp.com/site/info/version-support
2. Plan upgrade path to a supported version: Use the NetApp Upgrade Advisor Tool (UAT)
3. Review upgrade prerequisites: system node image show && storage failover show
4. Download the target ONTAP image from NetApp Support
5. Run ANDU pre-checks: system node image update -node * -package <url> -validate-only true
6. Schedule maintenance window and execute upgrade
7. For multi-hop upgrades, follow the supported upgrade path documented in the release notes
8. Update disk firmware and shelf firmware after the ONTAP upgrade
support_case_note: Open a support case for upgrade planning assistance, especially for multi-hop upgrades from very old versions.

name: Disk Firmware Not Current
description: Detects disks running firmware versions that do not match the recommended version in the firmware matrix. Outdated disk firmware can lead to drive failures, data integrity issues, and performance degradation.
check_query: SELECT d.system_id, d.name AS disk_name, d.model AS disk_model, d.firmware_version AS current_fw, f.recommended_version, f.latest_version FROM ontap_disks d INNER JOIN kb_firmware_matrix f ON f.platform = 'ontap' AND f.component_type = 'disk' AND f.model = d.model WHERE d.system_id = ? AND d.firmware_version != f.recommended_version AND d.state != 'failed'
condition: Disk firmware version does not match the recommended version in kb_firmware_matrix for the disk model
impact: Increased risk of disk failure due to known firmware bugs. Potential data integrity issues. Performance may be suboptimal. NetApp Support may require firmware update before investigating disk-related issues.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current disk firmware versions: storage disk show -fields firmware-revision,model
2. Download latest Disk Qualification Package (DQP) from https://mysupport.netapp.com/site/downloads/firmware/disk-drive-firmware
3. Download disk firmware package from NetApp Support
4. Stage firmware on the cluster: storage firmware download -node <node> -package-url <url>
5. Update disk firmware (non-disruptive for ADP/RAID-TEC): storage disk firmware update -node <node> -disk-list <disk1,disk2,...>
6. Monitor update progress: storage disk firmware update-status show
7. Verify updated firmware: storage disk show -fields firmware-revision -disk <disk>
8. Repeat for all nodes in the cluster
support_case_note: Open a support case if disk firmware update fails, if disks show I/O errors after update, or if a disk enters a failed state during the update process.

name: Shelf Firmware Not Current
description: Detects disk shelves running IOM/ESH firmware versions that do not match the recommended version. Outdated shelf firmware can cause shelf connectivity issues, path failures, and data access disruptions.
check_query: SELECT s.system_id, s.name AS shelf_name, s.model AS shelf_model, s.firmware_version AS current_fw, f.recommended_version, f.latest_version FROM ontap_shelves s INNER JOIN kb_firmware_matrix f ON f.platform = 'ontap' AND f.component_type = 'shelf' AND f.model = s.model WHERE s.system_id = ? AND s.firmware_version != f.recommended_version
condition: Shelf firmware version does not match the recommended version in kb_firmware_matrix for the shelf model
impact: Risk of shelf module failures. Potential loss of redundant path to disks. Shelf-to-shelf communication issues. In severe cases, entire shelf may become inaccessible, causing aggregate degradation.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current shelf firmware: storage shelf show -fields firmware-revision,module-type
2. Download shelf firmware from https://mysupport.netapp.com/site/downloads/firmware/disk-shelf-firmware
3. Stage firmware on the cluster: storage firmware download -node <node> -package-url <url>
4. Update shelf firmware (may require maintenance window for older shelves): storage shelf firmware update -shelf-name <shelf> -package-url <url>
5. Monitor update progress: storage shelf firmware update-status show
6. Verify updated firmware: storage shelf show -shelf <shelf> -fields firmware-revision
7. Note: IOM12/IOM12B updates are non-disruptive. Older IOM6/ESH modules may require a maintenance window.
8. Verify both IOM modules (A and B) are updated
support_case_note: Open a support case if shelf firmware update fails, if the shelf shows I/O errors post-update, or if IOM module status changes during the update.

name: SP/BMC Firmware Not Current
description: Detects Service Processor (SP) or Baseboard Management Controller (BMC) firmware that is not at the recommended version. The SP/BMC provides out-of-band management, remote console access, and hardware monitoring capabilities critical for remote troubleshooting.
check_query: SELECT e.system_id, e.node, e.message_name, e.message_text, e.time FROM ontap_ems_events e WHERE e.system_id = ? AND (e.message_name LIKE '%sp.fw%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%bmc.fw%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%sp.version%') AND e.severity IN ('warning', 'error', 'alert') AND e.time >= datetime('now', '-168 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate SP/BMC firmware version warnings or errors within the last 7 days
impact: Remote management capabilities may be limited. Hardware health monitoring may miss critical events. Remote console and power cycling may not function correctly. AutoSupport hardware data collection may be incomplete.
remediation_steps:
1. Check SP/BMC version: system service-processor show -node * -fields firmware-version,type
2. Check for available updates: system service-processor image show
3. Download SP/BMC firmware from NetApp Support if needed
4. Update SP/BMC firmware (non-disruptive): system service-processor image update -node <node>
5. Monitor update progress: system service-processor image show-update-progress -node <node>
6. Verify the update: system service-processor show -node <node>
7. Note: SP/BMC firmware updates are non-disruptive and do not affect data access
8. The SP/BMC will reboot during the update (1-2 minutes of remote management downtime)
support_case_note: Open a support case if SP/BMC firmware update fails or if the SP becomes unresponsive after the update.

name: Disk Qualification Package Outdated
description: Detects systems where the Disk Qualification Package (DQP) may be outdated. The DQP contains qualification data for all supported disk drives and is critical for proper disk recognition, performance tuning, and firmware compatibility.
check_query: SELECT e.system_id, e.node, e.message_name, e.message_text, e.time FROM ontap_ems_events e WHERE e.system_id = ? AND (e.message_name LIKE '%dqp%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%disk.qual%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%diskqual%') AND e.severity IN ('warning', 'error', 'alert') AND e.time >= datetime('now', '-168 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate DQP-related warnings or the DQP version does not match the latest available
impact: New disk drives may not be recognized. Disk performance tuning may be suboptimal. Disk firmware compatibility information may be outdated. Drive error handling may not benefit from the latest improvements.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current DQP version: storage disk option show -node * -fields disk-qual-package-version
2. Download latest DQP from https://mysupport.netapp.com/site/downloads/firmware/disk-drive-firmware/download/DQP
3. Install the DQP (non-disruptive): storage disk option modify -node * -disk-qual-package-path <url>
4. Verify update: storage disk option show -node * -fields disk-qual-package-version
5. Note: DQP updates are always non-disruptive and do not affect data access
6. Best practice: Update DQP before adding new disk shelves or drives
support_case_note: Open a support case if new disks are not recognized even after DQP update.

name: System BIOS Firmware Outdated
description: Detects nodes where the system BIOS firmware may not be at the latest version. BIOS firmware provides hardware initialization and platform-level functionality. Updates typically address security vulnerabilities and hardware compatibility.
check_query: SELECT e.system_id, e.node, e.message_name, e.message_text, e.time FROM ontap_ems_events e WHERE e.system_id = ? AND (e.message_name LIKE '%bios%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%firmware.update%') AND e.severity IN ('warning', 'notice') AND e.time >= datetime('now', '-168 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate BIOS firmware version is not current or BIOS-related warnings within the last 7 days
impact: Security vulnerabilities in BIOS may be exploitable. Hardware initialization issues may occur during reboots. Platform stability features may not be available. Compliance requirements for firmware currency may not be met.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current BIOS version: system node show -fields bios-version (if available)
2. Check system health for firmware alerts: system health alert show
3. BIOS updates are typically bundled with ONTAP upgrades
4. For standalone BIOS updates: Contact NetApp Support for the appropriate firmware package
5. BIOS updates require a node reboot: Schedule a planned takeover
6. Perform planned takeover: storage failover takeover -ofnode <node>
7. Update BIOS during maintenance window
8. Giveback after update: storage failover giveback -ofnode <node>
support_case_note: Open a support case to obtain the correct BIOS firmware package for your platform model. BIOS updates outside of ONTAP upgrades require NetApp guidance.

name: NIC Firmware Outdated
description: Detects network interface card (NIC) firmware that may be outdated. NIC firmware updates address network performance issues, link stability problems, and security vulnerabilities in the network stack.
check_query: SELECT e.system_id, e.node, e.message_name, e.message_text, e.time FROM ontap_ems_events e WHERE e.system_id = ? AND (e.message_name LIKE '%nic.fw%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%nic.firmware%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%eth.firmware%') AND e.severity IN ('warning', 'error') AND e.time >= datetime('now', '-168 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate NIC firmware warnings or version mismatch within the last 7 days
impact: Network link instability. Potential packet loss or CRC errors. Reduced network performance. Security vulnerabilities in the network data path.
remediation_steps:
1. Check NIC firmware versions: network port show -fields link,health-status
2. Check for firmware alerts: system health alert show
3. NIC firmware is typically updated during ONTAP upgrades via the Automated NIC Firmware Update feature
4. Verify NIC firmware update setting: system node image show -fields nic-firmware-auto-update-enabled
5. Enable auto-update if disabled: system node image modify -node * -nic-firmware-auto-update-enabled true
6. For manual NIC firmware updates, schedule a node reboot
7. Perform planned takeover: storage failover takeover -ofnode <node>
8. Apply NIC firmware update during the reboot cycle
support_case_note: Open a support case if NIC firmware auto-update fails or if network links are flapping after a firmware change.

name: Flash Cache Firmware Outdated
description: Detects Flash Cache (PAM II, Flash Cache 2/3) modules with firmware that may not be current. Flash Cache firmware updates address performance, reliability, and data integrity issues for the intelligent caching layer.
check_query: SELECT e.system_id, e.node, e.message_name, e.message_text, e.time FROM ontap_ems_events e WHERE e.system_id = ? AND (e.message_name LIKE '%flash.cache%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%flexscale%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%pam%') AND e.severity IN ('warning', 'error') AND e.time >= datetime('now', '-168 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate Flash Cache firmware warnings or version issues within the last 7 days
impact: Flash Cache performance may be suboptimal. Risk of cache module failure. Read acceleration is compromised if the module fails. Node performance degrades significantly without Flash Cache on read-intensive workloads.
remediation_steps:
1. Check Flash Cache status: system node flash-cache show -node <node>
2. Check Flash Cache firmware version: system node flash-cache show -node <node> -fields firmware-revision
3. Flash Cache firmware is typically updated during ONTAP upgrades
4. For standalone updates, download firmware from NetApp Support
5. Flash Cache firmware update requires a node reboot
6. Schedule planned takeover: storage failover takeover -ofnode <node>
7. Apply firmware during the reboot cycle
8. Verify after giveback: system node flash-cache show -node <node> -fields firmware-revision
support_case_note: Open a support case if Flash Cache module shows errors or if firmware update fails. Flash Cache hardware failures require field service replacement.
kb_articles: KB-1013911: Understanding WAFL cache and Flash Cache

name: ACP Firmware Outdated
description: Detects Alternate Control Path (ACP) firmware that may not be current. ACP provides an out-of-band management path to disk shelves, enabling shelf monitoring and firmware updates even when the primary data path is degraded.
check_query: SELECT e.system_id, e.node, e.message_name, e.message_text, e.time FROM ontap_ems_events e WHERE e.system_id = ? AND (e.message_name LIKE '%acp%' OR e.message_name LIKE '%alt.control%') AND e.severity IN ('warning', 'error', 'notice') AND e.time >= datetime('now', '-168 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate ACP firmware warnings or connectivity issues within the last 7 days
impact: Out-of-band shelf management is compromised. Shelf health monitoring may be limited. Background shelf firmware updates may fail. In case of primary path failure, shelf management is unavailable.
remediation_steps:
1. Check ACP status: storage shelf acp show
2. Check ACP firmware version: storage shelf acp show -fields firmware-version
3. ACP firmware is typically auto-updated by ONTAP
4. Verify ACP connectivity: storage shelf acp show -fields connectivity-status
5. If ACP is disconnected, check the ACP Ethernet cabling on the shelf
6. For manual ACP firmware update: storage shelf acp update-firmware
7. Verify update: storage shelf acp show -fields firmware-version
8. Note: ACP firmware updates are non-disruptive
support_case_note: Open a support case if ACP connectivity cannot be established or if ACP firmware update consistently fails.

name: Anti-Ransomware Protection Not Enabled
description: Detects read-write data volumes that do not have Anti-Ransomware Protection (ARP) or encryption enabled. ARP uses machine learning to detect anomalous volume activity that may indicate a ransomware attack, providing early warning and automatic snapshot protection.
check_query: SELECT system_id, name, type, is_encrypted FROM ontap_volumes WHERE system_id = ? AND type = 'rw' AND state = 'online' AND is_encrypted = 0 AND name NOT LIKE 'vol0%' AND name NOT LIKE 'MDV_%'
condition: Read-write online data volume has no encryption enabled (as a proxy for ARP configuration)
impact: No automated ransomware detection. Ransomware attacks may go undetected until significant damage has occurred. Recovery from ransomware attacks is more difficult without ARP-generated snapshots. Cyber insurance requirements may not be met.
remediation_steps:
1. Enable ARP in learning mode on the volume: security anti-ransomware volume enable -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -state dry-run
2. Allow ARP to learn normal workload patterns (minimum 30 days recommended)
3. Switch to active mode: security anti-ransomware volume enable -vserver <svm> -volume <vol> -state active
4. Configure ARP notification: security anti-ransomware volume event-log show
5. Enable volume encryption (NVE): volume encryption conversion start -vserver <svm> -volume <vol>
6. Alternatively enable aggregate encryption (NAE): storage aggregate modify -aggregate <aggr> -node <node> -encrypt-with-aggr-key true
7. Verify ARP status: security anti-ransomware volume show -vserver <svm>
support_case_note: Open a support case if ARP generates excessive false positives or if you need guidance on ARP tuning for specific workloads.
kb_articles: KB-1015410: ARP best practices and tuning

name: SSL/TLS Certificate Expiring Soon
description: Detects SSL/TLS certificates that will expire within 30 days by monitoring EMS certificate expiry warning events. Expired certificates will cause management access failures and disrupt encrypted communications.
check_query: SELECT system_id, node, severity, message_name, message_text, time FROM ontap_ems_events WHERE system_id = ? AND (message_name LIKE '%cert.expir%' OR message_name LIKE '%certificate%expir%' OR message_name LIKE '%ssl%expir%') AND severity IN ('warning', 'notice') AND time >= datetime('now', '-72 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate certificate expiry warnings within the last 72 hours
impact: HTTPS management access will fail when certificates expire. REST API integrations will break. SnapMirror encryption may be disrupted. LDAP over TLS will fail. AutoSupport HTTPS transport will stop working.
remediation_steps:
1. List all certificates: security certificate show -fields expiry-date,type,common-name
2. Identify expiring certificates: security certificate show -fields expiry-date -expiry-time-range <=30d
3. Generate new self-signed certificate: security certificate create -vserver <svm> -common-name <cn> -type server -expiry-time 365d
4. Or install a CA-signed certificate: security certificate install -type server -vserver <svm>
5. Update SSL configuration: security ssl modify -vserver <svm> -server-enabled true -client-enabled true -certificate <cert_name>
6. Delete expired certificates: security certificate delete -vserver <svm> -common-name <cn> -certificate-authority <ca> -type <type>
7. Verify new certificate: security certificate show -vserver <svm> -type server
support_case_note: Open a support case if certificate installation fails or if peering/SnapMirror encryption certificates need to be coordinated across clusters.

name: SSL/TLS Certificate Expired
description: Detects SSL/TLS certificates that have already expired by monitoring critical EMS events. Expired certificates cause immediate failures in HTTPS management, encrypted replication, and secure client access.
check_query: SELECT system_id, node, severity, message_name, message_text, time FROM ontap_ems_events WHERE system_id = ? AND (message_name LIKE '%cert.expired%' OR message_name LIKE '%certificate%expired%' OR message_name LIKE '%ssl%expired%') AND severity IN ('error', 'alert', 'emergency') AND time >= datetime('now', '-72 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate certificate has already expired within the last 72 hours
impact: HTTPS management access is broken. ONTAP System Manager is inaccessible via browser. REST API calls fail with certificate errors. SnapMirror encryption is disrupted. LDAPS authentication fails. AutoSupport HTTPS transmissions fail.
remediation_steps:
1. IMMEDIATE: Access cluster via SSH (not affected by certificate expiry)
2. List expired certificates: security certificate show -fields expiry-date
3. Generate replacement self-signed certificate: security certificate create -vserver <svm> -common-name <cn> -type server -expiry-time 365d
4. Or install a new CA-signed certificate: security certificate install -type server -vserver <svm>
5. Update SSL to use new certificate: security ssl modify -vserver <svm> -certificate <new_cert>
6. Delete expired certificates: security certificate delete -common-name <cn> -certificate-authority <ca> -type <type> -vserver <svm>
7. Test HTTPS access after replacement
8. Restart affected services if needed
support_case_note: Open a support case if you cannot access the cluster via any method due to certificate issues.
kb_articles: KB-1014920: Recovering from expired SSL certificates

name: FIPS 140-2 Compliance Not Enabled
description: Detects clusters where FIPS 140-2 compliance mode is not enabled. FIPS mode ensures all cryptographic operations use FIPS-validated modules, which is required for government and many regulated industry environments.
check_query: SELECT system_id, fips_enabled, tls_protocols FROM ontap_security WHERE system_id = ? AND fips_enabled = 0
condition: Cluster FIPS 140-2 mode is not enabled (fips_enabled = 0)
impact: Cryptographic operations may use non-FIPS-validated algorithms. Government and regulated industry compliance requirements are not met. Security audit findings may result. Weaker cipher suites may be negotiated for SSL/TLS connections.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current FIPS status: security config show -interface SSL
2. Review impact of enabling FIPS: Weak ciphers and TLS 1.0/1.1 will be disabled
3. Ensure all management tools support TLS 1.2+ with FIPS-compatible ciphers
4. Enable FIPS mode: security config modify -interface SSL -is-fips-enabled true
5. Verify FIPS is enabled: security config show -interface SSL -fields is-fips-enabled
6. Note: Enabling FIPS may disconnect management sessions using weak ciphers
7. Test all integrations (LDAPS, AutoSupport HTTPS, REST API clients) after enabling
support_case_note: Open a support case if enabling FIPS causes unexpected connectivity issues with management tools or integrations.

name: Audit Logging Not Configured
description: Detects clusters where audit logging is not enabled. Audit logs provide a forensic trail of administrative actions, security events, and configuration changes essential for compliance and incident investigation.
check_query: SELECT system_id, audit_log_enabled FROM ontap_security WHERE system_id = ? AND audit_log_enabled = 0
condition: Cluster audit logging is not enabled (audit_log_enabled = 0)
impact: No audit trail for administrative actions. Security incidents cannot be investigated forensically. Compliance requirements (SOX, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, GDPR) may not be met. Unauthorized changes cannot be tracked.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current audit configuration: security audit show
2. Enable CLI audit logging: security audit modify -cli-get true -cli-set true -ontapi-get true -ontapi-set true -http-get true -http-set true
3. Configure audit log destination: security audit log show
4. For NAS file access auditing, create audit configuration per SVM: vserver audit create -vserver <svm> -destination <path> -events file-ops,cifs-logon-logoff,cap-staging
5. Enable the audit: vserver audit enable -vserver <svm>
6. Configure log rotation: vserver audit modify -vserver <svm> -rotate-schedule-month all -rotate-schedule-dayofweek all -rotate-schedule-hour 0
7. Forward audit logs to SIEM: Configure syslog forwarding via event notification destination create
support_case_note: No support case required. Contact NetApp Professional Services for comprehensive audit logging architecture.

name: Default Admin Password Not Changed
description: Detects potential use of default admin passwords by monitoring EMS security events related to password configuration. Default passwords represent a critical security vulnerability that can lead to unauthorized access.
check_query: SELECT system_id, node, severity, message_name, message_text, time FROM ontap_ems_events WHERE system_id = ? AND (message_name LIKE '%passwd%default%' OR message_name LIKE '%security.login%default%' OR message_name LIKE '%mgwd.password%') AND time >= datetime('now', '-168 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate default password usage or password-related security warnings within the last 7 days
impact: Unauthorized access to the cluster is trivially easy. Complete control of storage infrastructure by attackers. Data theft, destruction, or ransomware deployment is possible. Regulatory compliance violations.
remediation_steps:
1. IMMEDIATE: Change the admin password: security login password -username admin
2. Change all default user passwords: security login password -username <user>
3. Review all local accounts: security login show
4. Remove unnecessary local accounts: security login delete -username <user> -application <app> -authentication-method <method>
5. Configure LDAP/AD authentication: security login create -username <user> -application ssh -authentication-method domain
6. Enable login banner: security login banner modify -message 'Authorized access only'
7. Configure account lockout policy: security login role config modify -role admin -max-failed-login-attempts 3
support_case_note: Open a support case if the admin account is locked and cannot be recovered through normal password reset procedures.

name: HTTPS Not Enforced
description: Detects clusters where HTTP (unencrypted) access may still be enabled for management. HTTP transmits credentials and management data in plaintext, making them vulnerable to interception and man-in-the-middle attacks.
check_query: SELECT system_id, tls_protocols, fips_enabled FROM ontap_security WHERE system_id = ? AND (tls_protocols IS NULL OR tls_protocols = '' OR tls_protocols LIKE '%1.0%' OR tls_protocols LIKE '%1.1%')
condition: TLS protocols configuration is missing, empty, or includes deprecated versions (TLS 1.0/1.1)
impact: Management credentials may be transmitted in plaintext. Man-in-the-middle attacks can intercept or modify management traffic. Compliance requirements for encrypted management access are not met. Security audit findings will result.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current HTTP/HTTPS settings: system services web show
2. Disable HTTP: system services web modify -http-enabled false
3. Ensure HTTPS is enabled: system services web modify -ssl-enabled true
4. Configure minimum TLS version: security config modify -interface SSL -supported-protocols TLSv1.2
5. Verify settings: system services web show && security config show -interface SSL
6. Test HTTPS access to ONTAP System Manager
7. Update monitoring tools and scripts to use HTTPS
8. Configure HSTS if supported by your management applications
support_case_note: No support case required.

name: SSH Using Weak Ciphers
description: Detects clusters where SSH is configured to accept weak or deprecated cipher suites. Weak ciphers (DES, 3DES, RC4, CBC-mode ciphers) are vulnerable to known cryptographic attacks and should be replaced with modern alternatives.
check_query: SELECT system_id, ssh_ciphers, tls_protocols FROM ontap_security WHERE system_id = ? AND ssh_ciphers IS NOT NULL AND (ssh_ciphers LIKE '%des%' OR ssh_ciphers LIKE '%3des%' OR ssh_ciphers LIKE '%rc4%' OR ssh_ciphers LIKE '%cbc%')
condition: SSH cipher configuration includes weak algorithms (DES, 3DES, RC4, or CBC-mode ciphers)
impact: SSH sessions may be vulnerable to known cryptographic attacks. Man-in-the-middle attacks may be possible. Compliance scanning tools will flag weak ciphers. Security audits will report findings.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current SSH cipher configuration: security ssh show
2. View available ciphers: security ssh show -fields ciphers
3. Configure strong ciphers only: security ssh modify -vserver <admin_svm> -ciphers aes256-ctr,aes192-ctr,aes128-ctr,aes256-gcm,aes128-gcm
4. Configure strong key exchange algorithms: security ssh modify -vserver <admin_svm> -key-exchange-algorithms diffie-hellman-group-exchange-sha256,ecdh-sha2-nistp256,ecdh-sha2-nistp384
5. Configure strong MAC algorithms: security ssh modify -vserver <admin_svm> -mac-algorithms hmac-sha2-256,hmac-sha2-512
6. Apply to all SVMs if needed
7. Verify: security ssh show -vserver <svm>
8. Test SSH connectivity with the updated cipher suite
support_case_note: Open a support case if SSH connectivity breaks after cipher changes, as some older SSH clients may not support strong ciphers.

name: AutoSupport Not Enabled or Failing
description: Detects systems where AutoSupport may not be functioning by checking for AutoSupport-related EMS error events. AutoSupport proactively sends diagnostic data to NetApp Support, enabling faster issue resolution and proactive risk identification.
check_query: SELECT system_id, node, severity, message_name, message_text, time FROM ontap_ems_events WHERE system_id = ? AND (message_name LIKE '%asup%' OR message_name LIKE '%autosupport%') AND severity IN ('error', 'alert', 'warning') AND time >= datetime('now', '-168 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate AutoSupport errors or failures within the last 7 days
impact: NetApp Support cannot proactively identify risks. Case resolution time increases without AutoSupport data. Risk-based alerting from Active IQ is unavailable. Hardware failure prediction capabilities are lost. SLA response times may be affected without active AutoSupport.
remediation_steps:
1. Check AutoSupport status: system node autosupport show -node *
2. Test AutoSupport: system node autosupport invoke -node <node> -type test
3. Check AutoSupport history: system node autosupport history show -node <node>
4. Enable AutoSupport: system node autosupport modify -node * -state enable
5. Configure HTTPS transport: system node autosupport modify -node * -transport https
6. Configure mail hosts if using SMTP: system node autosupport modify -node * -mail-hosts <smtp_server>
7. Verify proxy settings if behind a firewall: system node autosupport modify -node * -proxy-url <proxy>
8. Verify connectivity: system node autosupport check show
support_case_note: Open a support case if AutoSupport cannot reach NetApp despite correct network configuration, as firewall rules or proxy issues may need investigation.

name: AutoSupport Transport Not HTTPS
description: Detects AutoSupport configurations using SMTP or HTTP transport instead of HTTPS. HTTPS provides encrypted, reliable transmission of diagnostic data and is the recommended transport method for AutoSupport.
check_query: SELECT system_id, node, severity, message_name, message_text, time FROM ontap_ems_events WHERE system_id = ? AND message_name LIKE '%asup.smtp%' AND time >= datetime('now', '-168 hours')
condition: EMS events indicate AutoSupport is using SMTP transport within the last 7 days
impact: AutoSupport data may be transmitted unencrypted. SMTP transport is less reliable than HTTPS. Diagnostic data may contain sensitive configuration information. Mail server dependencies add failure points.
remediation_steps:
1. Check current transport: system node autosupport show -node * -fields transport
2. Change to HTTPS: system node autosupport modify -node * -transport https
